title: How Banks Make Money
summary: Banks make money primarily by charging more interest on loans than they pay on deposits. They also earn fees, process transactions, and use your deposits to fund lending at scale.

concepts.title: The Bank as a Middleman
concepts.summary: A bank sits between two groups: people with money they are not immediately using and people who need money they do not have. Depositors lend money to the bank for a small return; the bank lends that money to borrowers at a higher rate and keeps the difference. This spread — not the vault, the app, or the branches — is the core of the banking business model.

sections.title: What it is
body: Banks are financial intermediaries — institutions that sit between people who have money and people who need it. Before organized banking, anyone who needed to borrow had to find a willing lender on their own, and anyone with savings had no reliable way to earn a return on funds they weren't immediately using. Banks solved both problems simultaneously by creating a reliable place where capital could flow from those who have it to those who need it, at scale.

This middleman role is what makes banks useful to society. They collect small deposits from millions of people and pool that money into large loans that individuals could never arrange on their own — a $400,000 home loan funded by thousands of small savings accounts.

sections.title: How it works
body: A depositor brings money to the bank and receives a small interest payment in return for letting the bank use it. The bank pools that deposit with deposits from thousands of other customers and lends from that pool — mortgages, car loans, business credit. The borrower pays a higher interest rate than the depositor receives. The bank earns the difference. This difference — the spread — is the core of the bank's business model.

Trust is the invisible mechanism that makes this work. Depositors leave their money only if they genuinely believe it will be returned when requested. That trust is maintained by regulation, deposit insurance, audited financial statements, and institutional reputation. Without it, the system collapses: every depositor tries to withdraw at once, the bank fails because it has lent most of the money out, and the middleman function disappears.

sections.title: The numbers
body: If a bank pays depositors 2% per year on savings accounts and charges borrowers 7% per year on home loans, it earns a 5 percentage point spread on every dollar deployed. Scale that across $10 billion in loans: gross interest income of $700 million, deposit interest expense of $200 million, net interest income of $500 million before operating costs. The spread, not fee income or trading, is the dominant revenue source for most retail and commercial banks.
This simple structure explains why banks care so much about both sides of the relationship: lose depositors and the raw material disappears; lose borrowers and the revenue disappears.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: When you deposit $1,000 in a savings account, the bank does not hold your specific bills in a vault. It adds your deposit to a pool of funds and lends most of it out — to someone buying a car, a small business expanding its inventory, a homeowner refinancing a mortgage. When you want your $1,000 back, the bank pays you from new deposits flowing in or from its liquid reserves.

Your account balance is a legal claim on the bank, not a box of your own dollars. The constant flow of money in and out is what keeps the system working. The bank is always managing how much is coming in versus going out, and its profit depends on pricing that flow correctly.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: The middleman model has two direct consequences for consumers. First, it makes credit available on a scale that would otherwise be impossible — no individual saver could fund a $400,000 home loan, but a bank pooling millions of deposits can. Second, it means the bank profits from both sides: it pays you as little as possible on deposits and charges borrowers as much as competition allows.

Understanding this incentive structure is the starting point for making better decisions about where to keep money and where to borrow. The bank is not a neutral utility — it is a business with defined interests on both sides of every transaction you have with it.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept is the foundation of the entire topic. Every subsequent concept is a refinement or extension of the middleman model: the interest rate spread is how the bank prices the gap, fractional reserve banking is how it extends that gap across the economy, fees are an additional revenue layer, and regulation is what keeps the model from becoming reckless.

concepts.title: The Interest Rate Spread
concepts.summary: The interest rate spread — also called the net interest margin — is the gap between what a bank pays on deposits and what it charges on loans. It is the single most important number in banking. Three forces shape its width: market interest rate levels, competition from other banks, and the credit risk profile of borrowers.

sections.title: What it is
body: The interest rate spread is the gap between the rate a bank pays on deposits and the rate it charges on loans. It determines how much money the bank earns for every dollar deployed. A wider spread means more profit per dollar; a narrower spread means the bank must work harder with the same capital. Banks obsess over this number because most of their revenue depends on it.

Net interest income — the total dollar value generated by the spread across all assets — is the largest single revenue source for most retail and commercial banks. Fees, trading income, and advisory services are secondary.

sections.title: How it works
body: Three forces shape the spread. Market interest rate levels set the baseline: when the Federal Reserve raises the federal funds rate, banks can typically charge more on loans. If they raise loan rates faster than deposit rates, the spread widens. When market rates fall, the reverse can happen.

Competition constrains the spread from both sides. If a bank pays depositors 0.1% while a competitor offers 4%, depositors move their money. If it charges 9% on mortgages while competitors charge 6.5%, borrowers go elsewhere. Credit risk explains the variation in rates across borrowers: a low-risk borrower with excellent credit pays a lower rate; a higher-risk borrower pays more to compensate the bank for the elevated probability of default.

sections.title: The numbers
body: A bank with $10 billion in loans earning an average of 6% generates $600 million per year in interest income. If it funds those loans with deposits at an average cost of 1.5%, it pays $150 million in interest expense. Net interest income: $450 million. That $450 million must cover all operating costs — branches, staff, technology, compliance — before reaching profit. The net interest margin in this case is 4.5%.

This is why banks pay close attention to Federal Reserve rate decisions. A rate hike can expand margins if loan rates rise faster than deposit rates. A rate cut, or intense competition for deposits, can compress margins and force the bank to cut costs or lean more heavily on fee income.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: When the Federal Reserve raised rates aggressively in 2022 and 2023, many large banks saw net interest margins expand significantly because they could charge much more on new loans while deposit rates rose more slowly. Bank profits increased substantially during that period. Conversely, during the near-zero rate environment of 2010–2021, banks struggled to earn meaningful spreads and responded by reducing services, charging more fees, or taking on riskier lending to maintain profitability.
Banks actively manage both sides of the spread: adjusting what they offer on deposits, which loan products they promote, and how they price credit risk across different borrower profiles.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: The spread is the mechanism that determines the rates you are offered. When competition is fierce, banks compete for deposits by offering higher savings rates and for loans by offering lower rates. When competition is weak or margins are under pressure, the opposite happens.

Comparing rates across multiple institutions — not just accepting the first offer — is the most direct way to capture some of the spread value for yourself. You are not locked into any single bank's pricing, and the difference between banks on identical products is often larger than most people realize.

sections.title: Connections
body: The interest rate spread is the engine behind the middleman model. Regulation exists precisely because banks have strong incentives to chase wider spreads by taking on more risk than is socially optimal. And the spread explains why fees matter: when it compresses, banks look to fee income to fill the gap.

concepts.title: How Banks Are Regulated
concepts.summary: Banks are regulated because the risks they take with depositors' money are not contained within the bank. When a bank fails, depositors can lose savings, credit can freeze, and the damage spreads. Regulation manages this by requiring capital buffers that absorb losses first, and by insuring deposits so runs don't become self-fulfilling collapses.

sections.title: What it is
body: Banks are regulated because the risks they take with depositors' money are not contained within the bank itself. When a bank makes bad loans and fails, depositors can lose savings, businesses can lose access to credit, and the damage can propagate through the whole economy. The Great Depression illustrated this vividly: thousands of U.S. banks failed in the 1930s, wiping out ordinary savings and deepening an already severe economic collapse.

The regulatory system that exists today was built in direct response to those failures, and every major crisis since — including the savings and loan crisis of the 1980s and the 2008 financial crisis — has added new rules designed to prevent a repeat.

sections.title: How it works
body: Bank regulation works through several mechanisms. Capital requirements force banks to fund a minimum share of their assets with equity — money the owners have put in — rather than borrowed money. Equity acts as a cushion: if loans go bad, losses hit the equity first, before depositors are harmed. The more equity a bank holds relative to its assets, the more losses it can absorb before becoming insolvent.

Deposit insurance — in the United States, administered by the FDIC — guarantees that depositors will be repaid up to $250,000 per account even if their bank fails. This eliminates the incentive to panic: if your money is guaranteed by the federal government, there is no rational reason to rush to the window because of a rumor. Eliminating bank runs is enormously valuable because runs are often self-fulfilling — the act of running causes the very failure depositors feared. Liquidity requirements and stress tests add additional layers: the largest banks must demonstrate they could survive severe hypothetical downturns before regulators approve dividends or stock buybacks.

sections.title: The numbers
body: Capital requirements under the international Basel Accords require banks to hold equity equal to at least 8% of their risk-weighted assets, with higher minimums for the largest, most systemically important institutions. Risk weighting means a secured mortgage loan counts less heavily than an unsecured consumer loan — reflecting default risk, not just asset size.

During the run-up to the 2008 crisis, some institutions were leveraged 30 to 40 to 1, with almost no equity buffer remaining when asset values fell. Post-crisis reforms significantly increased required equity levels, and the 2023 collapse of Silicon Valley Bank led to further debate about whether deposit insurance limits and liquidity requirements need to be tightened further.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: The FDIC was created in 1933 in direct response to Depression-era failures. In the decades since, no depositor with an FDIC-insured account at a federally regulated bank has lost a penny of insured deposits due to bank failure. The 2008 crisis tested the system: Washington Mutual, the largest U.S. bank failure in history, was resolved over a single weekend with all insured depositors protected.

The crisis also produced the Dodd-Frank Act, which tightened capital requirements, created new oversight for large institutions, and established the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. Regulation is not static — it responds to the failures each crisis reveals.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: Regulation creates a floor of safety for ordinary bank customers. The FDIC's $250,000 insurance limit means those deposits are protected regardless of the bank's health — which should affect where you keep significant cash. Accounts at FDIC-insured institutions carry government-backed protection; accounts at uninsured alternatives do not.

Above the $250,000 limit, the safety of deposits depends on the financial health of the institution. Large depositors — businesses with payroll accounts, for example — must think carefully about institution quality in a way that smaller depositors generally do not.

sections.title: Connections
body: Regulation is directly linked to fractional reserve banking and how banks fund themselves, because both involve the core question of how much risk the banking system can safely carry. Capital requirements are the numerical answer regulators have settled on. This concept also explains why large bank failures generate policy responses that reshape the rules for all institutions.

concepts.title: Fractional Reserve Banking
concepts.summary: When you deposit money at a bank, the bank keeps only a fraction in reserve and lends the rest out. That lent money gets deposited elsewhere, lent again, and the cycle repeats — allowing the banking system to create far more credit than the deposits that actually exist. This money multiplication is what makes broad credit availability possible, and also why bank runs are so dangerous.

sections.title: What it is
body: When you deposit money at a bank, the bank does not lock it in a vault. It keeps a fraction in reserve and lends the rest out. This is fractional reserve banking — the mechanism that allows the banking system to create credit far in excess of actual deposits. A $1,000 deposit does not support just $1,000 in loans. Through the chain of deposits and re-lending that follows, it can support many times that in total lending across the system.

This is how banks transform a relatively small pool of savings into a much larger pool of credit available to businesses and consumers — and it is why banks are described as 'creating money' through the lending process.

sections.title: How it works
body: The mechanics unfold in a chain. You deposit $1,000. The bank keeps $100 in reserve (a 10% reserve ratio) and lends $900 to a borrower. That borrower spends the $900, and the recipient deposits it at another bank. That bank keeps $90 and lends $810. The $810 gets deposited again, and so on. Following the chain to its mathematical conclusion, the original $1,000 deposit supported up to $10,000 in total loans across the system. This is the money multiplier: 1 divided by the reserve ratio = 10 when the reserve ratio is 10%.

The system works because not everyone demands money at once. On any normal day, some depositors withdraw while others deposit, and the flows roughly offset each other. The problem arises when confidence collapses and an unusually large number of depositors simultaneously want their money — a bank run. Because most money has been lent out, the bank cannot satisfy everyone at once, and if it cannot borrow emergency funds quickly, it fails.

sections.title: The numbers
body: The Federal Reserve historically required banks to maintain reserve ratios of around 10%. In March 2020, the Fed reduced reserve requirements to zero, relying instead on capital rules and liquidity standards — requirements that banks hold enough high-quality liquid assets to cover 30 days of significant outflows — to ensure stability.

The theoretical money multiplier is a ceiling, not a guarantee. Banks often lend less than the maximum because loan demand is limited, credit standards are tight, or the bank holds excess reserves. The actual multiplier in the U.S. economy is substantially below the theoretical maximum.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: The 2023 collapse of Silicon Valley Bank illustrated fractional reserve vulnerability in a modern context. SVB held a large share of its assets in long-term government bonds that had declined in market value when interest rates rose sharply. When a subset of large depositors became concerned and began withdrawing simultaneously, the bank could not sell its bond holdings quickly enough without crystallizing massive losses. The resulting run overwhelmed the institution within 48 hours — even though the bank was technically solvent on paper.

This is why central banks stand ready as lenders of last resort: access to emergency central bank lending can stop a run before it becomes a failure, by assuring depositors that the bank can meet all withdrawal requests.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: Fractional reserve banking is why credit is available in the volumes modern economies require. Without it, every loan would need a matching deposit held idle — an impossibly inefficient system that would leave most mortgages, car loans, and business financing unfundable.

But it also means your deposit is not sitting in a vault. Your bank balance is a legal obligation the bank owes you, backed by a mix of reserves, loans, securities, and equity. FDIC insurance protects you from the practical consequences of this arrangement up to $250,000. Above that limit, the safety of your funds depends on the financial health of the institution.

sections.title: Connections
body: Fractional reserve banking ties directly to regulation — capital and liquidity requirements exist partly because the money-multiplying effect amplifies both the benefits and the risks of banking. It also connects to how banks fund themselves, because the liability side of a bank's balance sheet is essentially made up of the fractional deposits it has collected from millions of customers.

concepts.title: Fees and Charges
concepts.summary: Interest income is a bank's largest revenue source, but fees provide a second, rate-independent stream. Overdraft fees, monthly maintenance fees, ATM fees, and the interchange fees merchants pay when customers use cards all accumulate regardless of the rate environment. When the interest spread compresses, banks lean more heavily on fee income to maintain profitability.

sections.title: What it is
body: Interest income is the largest revenue source for most banks, but it is not the only one. Banks also generate substantial income through fees — charges for services, account maintenance, transactions, and specific behaviors. Overdraft fees, monthly maintenance fees, ATM fees, wire transfer fees, and the interchange fees merchants pay every time a customer swipes a card are all part of this revenue stream.

Fee income matters to banks because it does not depend on the interest rate environment. Whether rates are high or low, rising or falling, fees keep accumulating. When the interest spread compresses, fee income fills part of the gap.

sections.title: How it works
body: Different fees operate through different mechanisms. Overdraft fees are triggered when you spend more than your account balance and the bank covers the shortfall. The bank effectively extends a very short-term loan — sometimes just for a day — and charges a flat fee, historically $25–$35, for the service. Because the loan amount might be small and the fee large, the implied annual interest rate is extraordinary. These fees fall disproportionately on lower-income customers with thinner account balances.

Interchange fees flow through a less visible channel. Every time you pay by debit or credit card, the merchant pays a fee — typically 1.5% to 3.5% of the transaction — split between the card network and the card-issuing bank. The bank's share is interchange income. Credit card rewards programs are funded by this interchange: merchants who accept cards pay a premium on every transaction, some of which flows back to cardholders as rewards while the bank and network retain the rest.

sections.title: The numbers
body: For major U.S. banks, non-interest income — including fees, trading revenue, and advisory income — typically represents 30% to 50% of total revenue. Overdraft fee revenue at large U.S. banks peaked at roughly $17 billion per year before regulatory pressure and competition from fintech companies began reducing it. Several major banks have since reduced or eliminated overdraft fees.

Interchange fee regulation has created an asymmetry: debit card interchange is capped for large banks under the Durbin Amendment, while credit card interchange remains uncapped. This partly explains why credit card rewards programs tend to be more generous than debit rewards — the interchange pool funding them is larger and unregulated.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: The rise of digital-only banks — Ally, Marcus, Chime, and others — has put direct competitive pressure on traditional fee structures. These institutions often offer no-fee checking accounts and no overdraft fees because their lower operating costs (no branches) allow them to compete aggressively on price. Traditional banks have responded unevenly: some have dropped specific fees under competitive pressure; others have maintained them for customers who don't comparison shop.

The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau has periodically targeted overdraft fee practices, and regulatory attention has increased the political cost of maintaining high fee structures at large institutions.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: Fee income transfers money from your account to the bank in ways that may not be obvious when you first choose where to bank. A monthly maintenance fee of $15 on a checking account costs $180 per year before any overdraft charges. The most effective way to reduce what you pay in fees is to read the fee schedule before opening an account, maintain balances above any threshold that waives maintenance fees, and set up low-balance alerts to prevent overdrafts.
Choosing a bank or credit union with a genuine no-fee structure eliminates these costs entirely. The product is functionally identical to a fee-bearing account; the cost is not.

sections.title: Connections
body: Fees are the revenue diversification layer on top of the interest rate spread. When the spread compresses, banks lean more heavily on fee income. This concept connects directly to the final topic on practical consumer choices, because fee avoidance is one of the most direct ways to keep money in your account rather than the bank's income statement.

concepts.title: How Banks Fund Themselves
concepts.summary: To lend money, a bank must first obtain it. Banks fund themselves through three sources: customer deposits (cheapest and most stable), wholesale borrowing from financial markets (more expensive and less stable), and equity from shareholders (most expensive but the critical loss-absorbing buffer). Getting this mix right determines both profitability and resilience to stress.

sections.title: What it is
body: To lend money, a bank must first obtain money. Banks fund themselves through three main sources: customer deposits, borrowed funds from financial markets, and equity from shareholders. Each source has a different cost, a different stability profile, and a different role in risk management. The mix of these funding sources — the bank's capital structure — is one of the most consequential decisions a bank makes, because it determines both profitability and vulnerability to stress.
Unlike most businesses, a bank's raw material is money itself. What it pays to acquire funds is the direct determinant of what it must charge to lend them profitably.

sections.title: How it works
body: Deposits are the cheapest and most stable funding source. Customers accept low interest rates in exchange for convenience, safety, and FDIC protection. From the bank's perspective, deposits are liabilities — money it owes customers on demand — but because customers rarely withdraw en masse simultaneously, they function as a remarkably stable and cheap pool of capital.

Wholesale funding — borrowing from other banks, institutional investors, or through the bond market — is more expensive and less stable than deposits. Institutional lenders demand higher rates, and the funding can evaporate rapidly if confidence in the institution wavers. During the 2008 financial crisis, wholesale funding markets froze overnight: institutions stopped lending to each other because no one was certain which banks were solvent. Banks heavily reliant on wholesale funding — Bear Stearns, Lehman Brothers — collapsed when that funding disappeared. Equity is the most expensive source because shareholders require returns commensurate with risk, but it is also the most protective buffer — it absorbs losses before depositors or borrowed funds are touched.

sections.title: The numbers
body: A typical large commercial bank might fund $1 trillion in assets with roughly $700 billion in deposits, $200 billion in wholesale borrowing, and $100 billion in equity. The equity-to-assets ratio of 10% means the bank could absorb a 10% loss on its assets before equity is exhausted. The leverage ratio of 10 to 1 means every $1 of equity supports $10 in assets.

During the run-up to 2008, some investment banks operated at leverage ratios of 30 or 40 to 1, with essentially no margin for error. When asset values fell even modestly, equity was wiped out immediately. The Basel III reforms that followed significantly increased required equity levels for banks worldwide.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: The Basel III international capital standards, implemented after 2010, significantly increased the equity banks are required to hold. U.S. global systemically important banks must hold additional capital buffers above the standard minimum, creating meaningful cushion against system-wide shocks. Liquidity coverage ratios now require banks to hold enough high-quality liquid assets to survive 30 days of stressed outflows — a direct response to the funding crises of 2008.

A well-funded bank with a strong deposit base and ample equity can absorb loan losses and continue lending through a downturn. A thinly capitalized bank reliant on wholesale funding may cut lending entirely when stress arrives — amplifying economic damage — or require government rescue at public cost.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: The stability of a bank's funding structure affects how safe your deposits are and whether the bank will continue lending through economic difficulty. For most depositors at FDIC-insured institutions, the regulatory floor means the funding structure doesn't directly affect safety below the insurance limit. Above that limit, or when evaluating an institution outside the FDIC system, the quality of the capital structure matters.

The debate about how much capital banks must hold is a direct policy question about risk: more capital means safer banks and lower risk of crisis, but potentially tighter credit availability and lower returns for bank shareholders.

sections.title: Connections
body: How banks fund themselves is the structural foundation for both fractional reserve banking and the regulatory framework. Capital requirements are essentially rules about the minimum equity share in the funding mix. This concept also connects to the final topic: the type and quality of institution you bank with affects not only the rates and fees you pay but the stability of the institution holding your money.

concepts.title: Different Types of Banks
concepts.summary: Commercial banks, investment banks, and credit unions all operate under different rules, serve different customers, and make money in fundamentally different ways. Commercial banks take deposits and make loans. Investment banks serve corporations and institutions, earning fees from deals. Credit unions are member-owned cooperatives that return surplus to members rather than shareholders.

sections.title: What it is
body: 'Bank' covers institutions with very different business models. A commercial bank that holds your checking account, an investment bank that underwrites corporate bond offerings, and a credit union serving a specific employer's workforce are all called banks in everyday conversation — but they operate under different regulations, serve different customers, and make money in fundamentally different ways.
Understanding the distinctions helps you choose where to keep money, where to borrow, and — when following financial news — why different institutions behave differently across economic cycles.

sections.title: How it works
body: Commercial banks — Chase, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, and thousands of community institutions — take deposits from households and businesses and make loans. Their income comes primarily from the interest rate spread. They are regulated by the Federal Reserve, the OCC, or state banking regulators, and their deposits are FDIC-insured. This is the model described throughout this topic.

Investment banks — Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan's investment banking division — serve corporations, governments, and large institutions rather than individual consumers. They help companies raise capital by issuing stocks or bonds, advise on mergers and acquisitions, and trade financial instruments. Their revenue comes from fees on deals and trading profits, not deposit-loan spreads. Credit unions are member-owned cooperatives whose members collectively own the institution. Because they have no external shareholders requiring dividends, they return surplus revenue to members through lower loan rates and higher deposit rates. They are regulated by the NCUA and insured by the NCUA insurance fund, parallel to FDIC coverage.

sections.title: The numbers
body: The separation between commercial and investment banking was enforced for decades by the Glass-Steagall Act, passed in 1933. Its repeal in 1999 allowed banks to combine these functions under one roof. The mingling of consumer deposits and investment banking activities was one contributing factor in the 2008 crisis. Post-crisis reforms imposed restrictions on trading with depositor funds, though the fundamental structural separation was not restored.

For everyday comparison: credit unions consistently offer higher savings rates and lower loan rates than commercial banks of comparable size, because they are not extracting profit for external shareholders. Online-only banks offer competitive rates by passing operating cost savings — from not maintaining branch networks — directly to depositors.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: The most practically relevant comparison for most consumers is commercial banks versus credit unions versus online banks. Credit unions typically offer better rates but require membership eligibility and may have limited geographic reach. Online-only banks — Ally, Marcus, Discover Bank — offer competitive rates and FDIC insurance identical to traditional banks.

For businesses or individuals needing capital markets services — issuing equity, arranging bond financing, executing an acquisition — commercial banks cannot help. And investment banks do not accept consumer deposits. Knowing which type of institution serves your actual needs is the first step toward using each effectively.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: The type of institution you choose affects the rates you earn, the fees you pay, and the services available to you. If you qualify for a credit union with strong savings rates, the financial benefit over decades can be significant — credit union auto loan rates, for example, often run 1–2 percentage points below comparable commercial bank rates.

Most consumers would benefit from separating high-yield savings (online bank or credit union) from operational checking (wherever is most convenient) based on what each institution does best, rather than keeping all accounts at one institution by inertia.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept ties together the full picture of banking by showing how different institutions apply the same underlying ideas in different ways. Every earlier concept — the middleman model, the spread, regulation, fractional reserves, fees, and funding — applies specifically to commercial banks. Investment banks and credit unions operate on variations of the same model with different incentive structures. The final concept uses all of this to give specific practical guidance.

concepts.title: What This Means for You
concepts.summary: Understanding how banks make money translates directly into better financial decisions. Banks profit from the spread on your loans, the fees on your account, and the gap between what they earn on your deposits and what they pay you. Knowing these mechanics — and that competition gives you leverage — is what converts financial literacy into real savings.

sections.title: What it is
body: Understanding how banks make money translates directly into better decisions about where to keep money, where to borrow, and which fees to avoid. Banks are businesses. Their revenue depends on the spread between what they pay you on deposits and what they charge on loans, plus the fees they collect for various services. Every dollar of that spread and those fees represents money flowing from your account to theirs.

This is not an argument against using banks — it is an argument for using them strategically. The same regulatory protections, services, and FDIC insurance are available from institutions that charge less and pay more than the ones you might use by default.

sections.title: How it works
body: The key lever you have is competition. Banks compete for deposits and loans, which means you are not locked into any single institution's rates. A savings account paying 0.01% APY and one paying 4.5% APY both hold FDIC-insured dollars, but the difference in annual earnings on $10,000 is $449 per year. That difference comes from choosing, not from doing anything more complex.

Your behavior also determines how much fee income you contribute to the bank's revenue. Overdraft fees are avoidable with balance alerts and basic account management. Monthly maintenance fees can often be waived by maintaining a minimum balance or setting up direct deposit. ATM fees disappear when you use in-network machines or choose banks that reimburse them. None of these require financial sophistication — they require knowing the rules of the specific accounts you hold.

sections.title: The numbers
body: Moving $10,000 from a savings account earning 0.5% APY to one earning 4.5% APY earns $400 more per year. Over 10 years with compounding, the higher-rate account grows to approximately $15,530; the lower-rate account to approximately $10,511 — a difference of more than $5,000 from a single decision that takes minutes to act on.

On the borrowing side, the difference between a 6.5% and 7.5% mortgage rate on a $300,000 30-year loan is approximately $200 per month — roughly $72,000 in additional total interest paid over the life of the loan. Comparing rates across multiple lenders before accepting any offer is one of the highest-return uses of a few hours of time.

sections.title: Real-world impact
body: Online-only banks — Ally, Marcus by Goldman Sachs, Discover Bank — consistently offer savings rates several times higher than the industry average because they operate without branch networks and pass the cost savings to depositors. Their FDIC insurance is identical to any traditional bank. For household cash that doesn't need branch access, the choice between online and traditional savings is almost entirely a rate comparison with no meaningful downside to choosing the better rate.

The same logic applies to credit unions for borrowing. Credit unions consistently offer lower loan rates than commercial banks of comparable size because they are not extracting profit for external shareholders. If you qualify for membership at a well-run credit union, comparing its rates before taking a car loan or personal loan is worth the few minutes it takes.

sections.title: What this means for you
body: Three habits capture most of the available benefit. First, shop savings rates annually: the difference between average and best-in-class rates is often several percentage points, and moving money costs nothing. Second, minimize fees: read fee schedules before opening accounts, choose institutions with no maintenance fees on basic accounts, and set up alerts to prevent overdrafts. Third, compare loan rates from at least three lenders before accepting any offer — the variance across lenders for the same borrower is often enough to matter significantly over the life of the loan.
These habits require no financial sophistication, only the awareness that rates vary and that the variation matters — which is exactly what understanding how banks make money provides.

sections.title: Connections
body: This final concept ties together every earlier idea: the middleman model explains where the spread comes from; the spread concept shows why shopping rates matters; regulation ensures deposits are protected while you comparison shop; fractional reserve banking explains why the bank can lend so much from your deposit; fees show the second revenue layer to avoid; funding structure affects institutional stability; and the type of bank determines the baseline rate and fee structure you start from.
